title: feat: add community model publisher allowlist form
author: voodoohop
pr_body_excerpt:
- Adds a GitHub issue form for account-level community model publisher access—not individual model registration.
- Asks which model types the applicant plans to publish: text/chat, image, embeddings, speech, or transcription.
- Collects publishing plans, relevant links, hosting capacity, and required safety confirmations.
- Links the My Models UI and API docs directly to the publisher allowlist request.
- Clarifies that private models can be registered without allowlist approval.

Checks:
- YAML issue-form structure validation
- Biome on the My Models UI
- Gen TypeScript check
- API docs validation
- `git diff --check`

gist.headline: The garden gets gatekeepers
gist.blurb: Community model publishers now have a proper front door instead of a trail of scattered links. Tell the hive what you plan to grow, confirm the safety basics, and get ready to share.
gist.summary: Added a GitHub issue form for account-level community model publisher allowlist access, covering planned text/chat, image, embedding, speech, and transcription models, with direct links from My Models and API documentation.
gist.impact: Prospective publishers get a clear route to request public community-model access while private models remain available to register without allowlist approval.
gist.visual_concept: Depict the community-model publisher request as a cozy garden gate and sign-up desk: Polly, wearing a tiny librarian hat, helps a friendly creator fill out a pixel form listing different model seeds for chat, images, embeddings, speech, and transcription. A nearby private greenhouse is clearly open without a gate, while a CRT robot enthusiastically stamps approved gardening plans; code-plants and flowers make the publishing ecosystem feel welcoming rather than bureaucratic.
gist.image_prompt: A cozy pixel art garden-publisher desk with a lime green (#ecf874) garden gate and a chunky pixel form labeled "PUBLISH MODELS," showing tiny seed icons for chat, image, embeddings, speech, and transcription; an open little greenhouse nearby is labeled "PRIVATE MODELS." The main character is a cute pixel art bee mascot with a yellow body and dark brown horizontal stripes, small black legs, two black antennae, black vertical oval eyes, bright pink rectangular blush marks on cheeks, a small happy smile, and small rounded light blue translucent wings; she wears a tiny librarian hat and warmly helps a creator submit the form while a cute CRT-monitor robot stamps a plan beside blooming code-plants. Cozy 8-bit pixel art aesthetic, soft pastel gradients, warm lighting, retro gaming vibes, like Stardew Valley or A Short Hike, nostalgic but beautiful.
